. CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The half-yearly meetings of the Great Sick and Sour-
face Railway, and of the Great Northern Bigot line,
were held on the one day last week—by accident, we
presume! At the meeting of the latter Rail-
way, Mr. Dolan, of  Ardee, moved that the fol-
_lowing be added to the Report :—* That the report be
adopted with the addition that the directors be re-
quested to prepare a scheme for throwing entrance to
our clerkships open to compoetitive examination, some-
what after the manner of the Civil Service and the Great
Southern and Midland Great Westorn Railway Com-
panies.”’ Speaking in favour of this addition to the
Report, Mr. Michael Sullivan pointed out that a large
goction of the people were at present excluded from com-
petition for the clerkships. He added, according to a
" Press report, ‘But if opposition to the proposal in
question should be continued, be should be driven to
the conclusion that there was something in the backs of
the minds of those who opposed it.”” Quite so; and as
the proposal was opposed by the ruling powers, it is
aagy to see what was in the back of their minds; it was
simply bigotry. They want to keep the Papists out

b

As our readers know, there is a kind of a sort of an
examination held in connection with the clerkships of
this notorious company, and we are glad that as a re-
sult of the questions asked at the meeting the public
have been given some definite information conceruing
these make-believe examinations. The Chairman said
that the candidates for clerkships were examined by per-
sons in the employment of the company who knew what
was required. What a pretty examination, to be sure!
We agree that the examiners do know what is required.
‘When they have found out where the candidate nad
been educated, they know very often, no doubt, what
is not required. The amendment was negatived, and
the Chairman declined to grant a poll. This is the
first time, if we recollect rightly, that a formal request
was made to have the clerkships thrown opem to com-
petitive examination; needless to say, the effort will be
persisted in until this reasonable reform—a reform
desirable from every point of view except the bigots’
point of view—is conceded.

Our readers will have, no doubt, noticed that Mr.
John Dillon did not lift his voice in behalf of this
reasonable and just demand. Perhaps the couple of
hundred Orangemen who are alleged—very much
alleged—to have voted for Mr. Devlin in West Belfast
might get cross with him if he gave a whisper, not to
say lifted his voice, in favour of competitive examina-
tions for clerkships on the Great Northern Railway. A
specially-contributed article in this issue deals further
with the G. N. Railway.

At Kingsbridge on the same day the affairs of the
much-depreciated property of the G. S. and W. share-
holders were subjected to a good deal of criticism. In
the course of his remarks Mr. Sexton very properly
referred to the manners displayed at the last meeting
by the self-styled “ geuvieman’’ who presides over the
railway, but who never had the courage to answer the
question whether or not he had contracted to give his
whole time to the manure. Mr. Sexton said:— But
you spoke of it as a difference of opinion between your-
self and me, and you did so in a passage which it would
be hard to match either as a specimen of reasoning or
as a display of taste.”’ We trust that Brother Gould-
ing was not too much a “ gentleman,’’ as he might say
himself, to appreciate this reference to his notorious
taste.

As was to have been expected, Brother Goulding
gave no explanation why the contract at the Waterford:
station was given to a “saved’’ firm at some three
thousand pounds higher than the tender of a well-
known firm that have the economic disadvantage of
being “Idolators.’”” ~All that the Grand Steward of
the Secret Society, the Free and Accepted Masons of
Ireland, said was that the Board went into the matter
with their skilled advisers, and on their recommendation
the Board accepted the tender of the “saved ” firm as
against a tendor somo £3,000 less from an * Idolatrous’
firm. It is almost needless to point out the “saved
got the contract “in the interests of the company!
No doubt it was in the interest of the higher civilisa-
tion that the man Coote saved Carnthall Society from
the machinations of an ‘Idolatrous’’ girl sewing-
instructress! 5 2

The spiciest if not the most Important matter brought
before the meeting of the Great Sick and Sour was the
supply of soda water and minerals which was taken from
a leading firm and given to & firm by the name of Kiloh
and Co. Mr. John Gore was anxious to know whether
the self-styled ‘ gentleman” would account for the

giving away of something like £4,000 to a firm «of con-
tractors while another fixm was quite as competent to do
the work and at a lower price. The “ Grand Stewavd *’
4 of the Fl‘ee_mflsons in this country brushed the imper-
tinent and “ irrelevant ’’ question aside by announcing
that “ as regarded Mr. Gore’s remarks he was informed
that no such contracts existed with Messrs, Kiloh for
the sale of mineral waters. The Board had nover inter-
fered with Mr. Koenigs as regards stores. Al that they
insisted upon was that the goods supplied to the public
should be the very best possible kind.”” That explana-
tion Is very innocent and bland, for no person can be
more innocent and bland looking than Brother Goulding
“ gentleman,’” when he likes. :

Waiving the question of the existence of a contract,
the fact is not controverted that Kiloh and Co. are sup-
plying the mineral waters now. And who, pray, are
Kiloh and Co.? Kiloh himself is, we understand, a
clerk of Brother Goulding’s, and manages the drug and
apothecaries’ shop in Patrick Street, Cork, from which
the noble house of Goulding sprung. Some years ago
Goulding, probably experiencing a premonition of future
nobility, and considering that he was too much of a
““ gentleman *’ to run a drug shop, Mr. Kiloh’s name was
put over the apothecaries’ door. “Kilohs " developed
the mineral water making business, and they also, we
understand, went into the bungery business. ~ A North
of Ireland man by the name of Bannister, who preceded
Kiloh, was a strong temperance advocate and protest-d
against the drink traffic and resigned. Kiloh tock ‘s
place as manager and a company was formed u.l i
name.

Though it may be true that there is no contract wilh
Kiloh and Co., it is not denied that the supply of mine-
ral waters came from that place any way. We hear that
a big Dublin mineral water firm are so naturally indig-
nant at this piece of business that they have ordered
that all their goods to Limerick are to be sent via Broad-
stone, and this means, we understand, a diversion of
something-like £20 weekly traffic. ~ Was it prophetic
that Mr. Kiloh has an oh! after his name?

A Mr. Edward Cahill, of Cork, almost invariably in
recent times speaks at the general meetings of the Great
— Sick-and Sour. According to a newspaper report this
Mr. Cahill said at the last meeting:—“ As to Sir Wil-
liam Goulding’s connection with the Cork firm, he
wished to say that he wished everyone else would take
his example and encourage industries in Ireland. He
did not think that gentlemen like Mr. Sexton should
continue this factious agitation.”” Now, who is this Mr.
Cahill, of Cork, who deprecates “this factious agita-
tion.””  Well, some years ago, great commercial autho-
rity though he may be, he paid 2s. in the £ to his cre-
ditors. He carries on a tin shop on Pope’s Quay. Pos-
sibly he considers that by backing up Brother Goulding
that that “ gentleman ’’ may be induced to speak to M.
“Pursey Burnard,” who in turn might speak to his
cousin, Lord Bandon, who again might cause Mr. Cahill
to be made what in Cork is contemptuously called a
““ Coal Quay J.P.”

Mr. Stanley Harrington, who is a divector of Brother
Goulding’s Manure Company, and likewise one of our
stalwart Commissioners of “ National ”’ Education, was,
on the proposition of a Mr. Staunton, Cork, seconded
by a Mr. Coppinger, Cork—whoever they are—ap-
pointed auditor of the Great Sick and Sour. Oh, it is
a happy family! Perhaps the next vacancy that occurs
on the Board Brother Goulding will direct that his man
Kiloh bo co-opted! Or, better still, why not all the
directors resign and let the show be run by Brother
Goulding, the rest of the directors being men from the
knacker’s yard.

The violently sectarian and bigoted paper run by the
¢ Grand Master "’ of the ‘‘ Independent ’’ Orangemen,
one Mr. Crawford, styled by Mr. Willie .Red_mond a
“Jiberal Protestant,”” in the course of its editorial notes
last week says:—

When the priests cease to be presidents of the United Irish
League branches throughout the country, and when the Roman
Catholic Hierarchy are forced to abstain from using the Home
Rule P for fighting the battles, not of Ireland, but of the
Italian Propaganda, Mr. Redmond may claim wx_t]\ some degree
of truth that his Party is both democratic and national.

We trust Mr. Redmond’s younger brother, Mr.
William, appreciates this ‘‘liberal”” ultimatum from
this ‘ liberal Protestant.’”

Our readers may be interested in a few more samples
from the sectarian paper of this ‘‘ liberal Protestant,”
as Mr. Willie Redmond styled this Orangeman: —

Tn other words, does Mr. Redmond stand on the side of Mr.
M chael Davitt on the question of national education, or will the
Irish Party be once more whipped to heel by Bishop O'Dwyer and
the Maynooth prelates? We know of only one ideal of democracy,
and only one creed of nation d thoy stand for the supremacy
of the people in the affairs of

Notwithstanding the recent bungling of Mr. Davitt,
the paper of this Orangeman backs him up. We read:

Dr. O'Dwyer has come badly out of the controversy with that
ran leader of Roman Catholic Democracy, Mr. Michael Davitt.

Lven a less petulant and self-opinionated person than
““ that veteran leader of Roman Catholic Democracy ™
might well be induced to examine his conscience on
being the recipient of such a compliment from this
Orange paper.

Monsignor ITallinan presided at the annual moeting
of the Newcastle West Branch of the Gaclic League.
Judging by his address the League in that town is in a
vigorous condition.  The only other branches in the
district that had been carnestly working during the year
wore, he said, the Castlemahon and Feohenagh
Branches, and the latter two owed theiv success to the
exertions of Father Liston and the Committees. The
attendance at the classes of the Newcasile West Branch
every night this year had been in advance of what it had
been any year since the formation of the branch. But
Monsignor Hallinan was sorry to find that there was as
yeb a good number of the young women and men of the
parish who had not commenced the study of Irish not-
withstanding all the inducements which comfortable,
well-lighted, warm rooms, frec tuition, ete., held out
to them. The work in tho schools was progressing
satisfactorily also, he said, and his hope for the ultimate
succoss and revival of the Irish tongue rested principally
on the rising generation, and on the work which was
carried on in the schools. Monsignor Hallinan also ap-
pealed to the shopkeepers to put up their names in Irish
over their premises, and trusted that they would also
consider their national duty to stock Irish-made goods,
and press the sale of them by every means in their
power. It would, indeed, be well if every branch of the
League in Ireland had as good a yearly tale to tell as
had that of St. Ita’s, Newcastle West.

FEBRUARY 24, 1906.

Our readers will sympathise with the appeal from the
Capuchin College, Rochestown, Co. Cork. Rochestown
College has carned an honoured name in Irish Ireland.
Several students from this College gained honours in
Irish in the Intermediate in the yoars 1904 and 1905,
and every boy presented in Irish passed. Three-quar-
ters of an hour of every morning of the six working
days of the week are devoted to the teaching of Irish,
and every boy in the place is learning Irish. Irish
games are played, and Inish is spoken as much as pos-
sible during the playing of them. The fact that the
Rector of the College is Father Augustine will gain for
the appeal which is being issued on behalf of the Col-
lege a particular interest from the many personal
friends of that stalwart champion of the Irish Ireland
movement.

On last Thursday night the third annual Irish musical
festival, organised by the Gaelic League of Lanarkshire,
Scotland, was held at Motherwell Town Hall. The
programme of this Irish concert in Scotland was printed
in Ireland, and on the frout of it we read:—mda'p
CGipeannac €, Labap teanga na Gipeann  agup
Tabaiy congnam 0o Connpad na Jaeovilge cum i
¢oim»a0 beo. The plucky and sustained manner in which
the Irish Irelanders in Lanarkshire stick to their work
is an example that ought to make the people of many
Trish towns feel humble enough in their own estimation.

Cappoquin is in the heart of an Irish-speaking
country. There was to have been a “ grand vocal and

instrumental concert ’’ there on February 15, but it
was postponed. There was, of course, to have been a
“ sparkling array of local talent.”” Some Johnnie or
other was to have sung “ Whirligig M‘Gee, Esquire.”
Another song (in character) was to have been “The
Donegal Mashers,”’ and a ‘“ humorous’’ song, “Fiz,
Glorious Fiz.”” According to the projected programme
for this concert that did not come off, there was to have
been no song in Irish, and the ““ grand '’ entertainment
was to have wound up with a Scotch lyric, “ Auld Lang
Syne.” [ 2

There was what we believe is called a “ triple bill

at the Abbey Theatre on Monday night. We were not
in for all of ““the Hour Glass,”” a morality play by Mz:.
W. B. Yeats. This play was dealt with in thesc
columns on the occasion of its first production, so that
we need not refer to it at length; all we need say is
that the declamation of Mr. F. J. Fay and Miss Sarah
Allgood, in the principal parts, was excellent.  The
third piece was “ Kathleen ni Houlihan.”” It was the
second time we saw this play, and it improved o
acquaintance.
“ Hyacinth Halvey *’ is the curious name for a very
amusing new play by Lady Gregory. Hyacinth’s
mother must have been suffering from very “grand’
notions if it was she who selected the name for her
unfortunate son; if we were to suggest the names that
she would probably have decided upon, if her hopeful
turned out to be a daughter, and not a son, some of
our “highly respectablo " acquaintances might consider
that we were indulging in personalitics. Iyacinth will
never stick in our mind for young  Misther " Halvey—-
Jerry or Petor would fit his suit of highly respectable,
ready-made clothes better. Ilowever, whal’s in a
name? IHyacinth might reply to that question in the
jdentical way that he would reply to tho question—
what’s in a character? A great deal. For Hyacinth suf-
fered from what we may call a plethora of good
character.  The character that his testimonials gave
him was too great a burden for any young man of spirit
to live up to.

The play is amusing from start o finish; it is full of
action ; and it is constructed ingeniously, and openly lays
full contribution on stage devices, Tho two Fays are
the life of the play. W. G. is a dishonest butcher who
deals largely in diseased meat, an unscrupulous trade
that would suit the villian of melodrama whom the

THE. LEADER.

3

virtuous gallery would hiss, better than it did James
Quirke the butcher of Cloon, whom everyono in the
audionco—even if he were a Sanitary Inspector—would
have to laugh with. Is it part of the comedy that
people of unimpeachable rectitude are compelled to laugh
with a vendor of sheep that died a natural déath? There
was Mr. T. P. Gill prominent amongst the audience
giving scandal by laughing at this unscrupulous butcher
whose meat, if it was as contagious as his humour,
should certainly be buried at dead of night by a De-
partment “ Expert.’’ S

Hyacinth Halvey sought and secured the post of Sub-
Sanitary Officer of Cloon. His testimonials’ were
true comedy of Irish life. But when he got the job the
dead-weight of tho testimonials lay upon him. THe was
a paragon, and some people of Cloon, alas, expected poor
Ilyacinth to live up to his testimonials. A pious maiden
lm}ly secured him lodgings next the police barrack, and
opposito the priest’s house! They were lodgings in
strict keeping with his “ testimonials.”’ Poor Hyacinth
protested on the quiet that he was not as harmless as he
looked ; but the testimonials’’ stamped him as a
prodigy of every virtue.

The situation is developed with ingenuity, and a free
use of conventional stage craft. Hyacinth decides to
do something wicked in order to shake off the dreadful
burden of his good character. He steals the carcase of
a sheep and confesses his crime to the dishonest butcher ;
but, alas, the crime only added to his good character.
For as the police had just been ordered to search Mr.
Quirke’s shop for diseased meat, the stealing of the only
carcase in the shop took the unscrupulous butcher out
of serious trouble, and so Quirke, as it were, gilded
refined gold by adding to the already more than bearable
zood character that the testimonials had saddled on poor
Hyacinth.  His effort to throw off his character by
stealing from the box in the Protestant church was of
no avail either. TFor when the telegraph messenger is
found with a marked half-crown, Hyacinth confesses
that it was he broke into the church, but the policeman
and the rest take the confession as a heroic if mis-
guided act of self-sacrifice by way of saving the telegraph
messenger, and the unbearably good character of Hya-
cinth goes up further! Even the policemen tell Hya-
cinth that his attempt to shield the telegraph boy has
taught them all a lesson. Hyacinth cannot shake off
his good character; all his efforts only add to the un-
bearable burden.

The play is amusing from first to last; the dialogue
is sprightly and clever throughout; the two Fays act
excellently, and Miss Allgood, though she makes a better
Kathleen ni Houlihan than a peasant woman, or rural
post-office keeper, did her part very well. The “triple
bill ”” may be witnessed on Friday and Saturday, and
our readers should not let the opportunities slip. To-
night (Thursday) “ Riders to the Sea ' will be the cur-
tain raiser to the “ Eloquent Mr. Dempsey ’’; and M.
Dempsey is very well worth seeing.

dorson, 41 Mount Nod Road, Streatham, London, mar-
ried woman; Marie Eloanor Piper, Benendon, Cron-
brook, Kent, spinster; Edward Kirwood Adams, 3
Stockfield Road, Streatham, London, S.W., bank clerk
L. Rogers Adams, 8 Stockfield Road, Streatham, Lon-
don, 8.W., married woman. Here you have a Kent

|5Esd spinster and a London bank clerk amongst those exploit-
ing Carrick gas! We have not heart to make the ob-
vious joke about the efficiency of Ireland in the produc-
tion of gas. The first directors of this Carrick company
are—Thomas Harold Benger, Thomas Brown Younger,
Robert Bruce Anderson. We aro sure the London bank
clerk and the Kent spinster would laugh immpoderately
if they heard what they would probably call the
*“ wheeze *’— Caxrick I dread you.””

conduct.

On next Monday night the dance organised by -the
Rathmines and Rathgar Catholic Association will be
held in the D.B.C. rooms, O’Connell Strect. We are
informed that the occasion promises to be a very enjoy-
able one.

Mr. M. Hughes, of Hughes’ Academy, Belfast and
Londonderry, has this week opened a branch academy
at 122 Stephen’s Green, Dublin, for Civil Service and
commercial training. As a special inducement to .join
his classes he is offering to all students enrolling on or
before Thursday, 1st March, the advantage of being
admitted to tuitions at half fees. We have received
“The Civil Service Tutor,’”’ published by Mr. Hughes,
containing lists of successes and acknowledgments from
several past students of the efficiency of his teaching.

One Thomas Ritchie, a rowdy from Trinity College,
Dublin, was only fined 40s. or a month in jail for his
part in the blackguardism at the Royal Theatre last
Saturday night. If it had boen a few National
youngsters influenced by some political motive who had
created a disturbance how 4/f Foz and Lord Bung’s
Bzpress would thunder out denunciations, and weep
hot tears over the decadence of manners. But it was
a Trinity cad. What actien do the Government intend
taking with a view to curbing the rowdyism of Trinity
College ragamuffins?

FEBRUARY 24, 1906.

Frederick Oliver Trench, better known as I_nord A.“h
town, is a notorious bigot. A letter from this fa.u.at,'lc
to one C. E. Cooper, of Birkcu-h-a;'xd, is published in
Saturday’s issue of Lord Bung’s Daily ‘ﬁ‘rprcss, ‘While
such men as Lord Ashtown are profvhl\g about, there
will bo plenty of need for the Catholic Defence Society.
The lotter, though long, is worth reproducing in full.
Tt is a partial self-revelation of the mind and attitude
of an anti-Irish and anti-Catholic bigot, and as such
conveys as much as hundreds of facts of bigotry; it
shows the mental attitude that produces the overt acts
of bigotry with which our readers are familiar.

Woodlawn, County Galway.

Dear Mn. Coolem,--Many thanks for your kind let'er. Yes, I
am very keen on  tho Protestant cause,” as wo poor Prote:tants
in Iroland feel the full power of the Church of Rome. I have done
my little best to try and open .the cyes of the English Protestants
as to the aotion of Rome in Ircland. I tried to help by sending
out a emall book called *“Grievances from Ireland,” which you
take in. I hopo that vou may direct the English Protestants’
attention to our case.

As you know, and most likely all the English people know, the
Nationalist cause (so-called) is worked by the United Irish League.
who piok the M.P.’s. Now, what I want to bring home to the
Englich Protestants is, that this League is run by the Churoh of
Rome, whoso object is not to help the Irish people, but to
strengthen the Church of Rome

If you want proof of this, look through your back numbers of

“2UTRID FILTH” AT THE DUBLIN “GAIETY.”

THING by the name of ““ The Spring Chicken **
A was the attraction at the Gaiety Theatre, Dublin,
during last week. It was heralded by the usual puffs
in the Evening Telegraph and the FEvening Herald.
The ‘“truly Catholic’’ Freeman advertised it, so did
the Independent. We paid a shilling and went to the
pit, at the matinee performance, on Saturday. The
majority of the people visible from the pit were women ;
young women, old women, even children were there.
We found- the thing painfully dull for the most part;
here and there a piece of good farcical acting enlivened
it somewhat; but the audience apparently enjoyed the
thing immensely; it was just the sort of thing they
liked. We charitably assume that many, if not most,
of those present were only semi-conscious of the kind
of thing they were patronising. The class of * fashion-
able’’ young and old women from the suburbs
who pay their shillings to enter the pit at a
matinee of a ‘‘ musical comedy *’ are mot a thoughtful
class of people. They are a collection made up of a
skirt and a blouse, with a cheap-sale hat on top of them.
We are willing to look with charitable eyes on some oi
these hats, blouses, and skirts from the ‘‘highly-
respectable *’ villas of Clonskeagh, Dalkey, and Pem-
broke, who trooped in at a shilling per hat—it would
be somewhat overstating the case to say per head—to
enjoy ‘‘ The Spring Chicken.”’” Many of the young and
old ladies present might, in extenuation of their pre-
sence at the filthy piece, plead that they had not
properly come to the use of reason.

We are not going to say much ourselves about this
‘“ comedy.”’ We wrote pretty plainly in the past about:
‘“ Zaza,” ‘‘ Kitty Grey,”” ‘‘ Sapho,”” and such abomina-
tions. We prefer to let Mr. Stead, of the Review of
Reviews, have his say on ‘‘ The Spring Chicken.”” Mr.
Stead being an Englishman, his opinion may have more
weight with the directors of the Gaiety Theatre and
with a cortain non-Irish as well as nondescript portion
of the public than we mere Irish people might be cal-
culated to have.

Mr. Stead headed his article ‘ Gomorrah at the
Gaiety ”’—the Gaiety referred to was that of London
where this abomination of the Edwardes’ Company first
appeared. Here is an extract from Mr. Stead’s view
of this " filth heralded to Dublin by puffs in our
‘“ national ’’ evenings, the T'elegraph and the Herald: —
{“As I came out I could not help recalling the ghastly
jest of Mr. Punch, who represented one poor, wretched,
draggle-tailed street walker accosting another as forlorn
with the question, ‘How long have you been gayf’
For the gaiety of the Gaiety Theatre is as the gayness
of the gay women on the streets, as hollow and as base.”’
We wonder if this quotation should reach the eyes of
any of the crowds of the young and old ‘‘ fashionable **
ladies who trooped to ‘‘ The Spring Chicken '’ will there
bo enough virtue in them to cause them a pang of shame?

‘We quote again from Mr. Stead: —When T left the
theatre T was appalled to think that such a performance
can be ‘applauded nightly by thousands of well-dressed
English people without a ‘word of protest from the
Press.””  Well, crowds of well-dressed people from
Dirty Dublin patronised it too, and the Telegraph and
the Herald welcomed it

We quote Mr. Stead again:—* Tho play is no doubt

" an adaptation from the French, but not even the lax
and indifferent society of Paris would allow such a play
to be performed before a theatre half full of girls.”
That is rather hard on the young and old ladies of
Filthy Dublin, who trooped to the Gaicty last weel.
Mr. Stead talks of the ‘ promisenous animalism which
reigns supreme on the stage of the Gaiety.” Again:—
““ The whole musical comedy is one long presentation
of lust, unredeemed by a single spark of sentiment. The
whole thing is reduced to the level of monkeys at the
Zoo.” We trust that the fashionable young and old
ladies, full no doubt of sentiment and grandeur, who

E trooped to the Gailety last week, will enjoy that extract

! from Mr. Stead’s article.

Mr. Stead says of one of the characters: —‘‘ He locks
himself into his office with frail clients, and accompanies
them to restaurants of ill-fame.””

Here is another quotation: —‘‘ We have heard a good
| deal of the comic dramatists of the Restoration. But
| T doubt whether Wycherly or Congreve ever compressed
into any of their comedies a more compact mass of dirty
allusions and adulterous suggestions than those which

pretty young girls make on the Gaiety stage for the

* edification of the British public.”” Evidently ‘dirty

| allusions ’* and ‘‘ adulterous suggestions ’’ on the stage

l are popular amongst fashionable ladies of Dirty Dublin,

I

i

who flock to Gaiety musical ‘‘ comedies.”

- We quote again from Mr. Stead:—‘‘ But how comes
it that this prudish, proper, virtuous English society has
not a word to say in condemnation not of a play of
illicit love—for there is not a scintilla of love to irra-
diate the putrid filth—but the glorification of libidinous-
ness.””  Who are the shareholders of the Gaiety Theatre,
Dublin, who traffic in ‘‘ putrid filth,”” and is it not a
h nice state of affairs that the Herald and the Telegraph
I give preliminary puffs to ‘‘ putrid filth’’?  Another
phrase from Mr. Stead’s article is *‘ this pestilent and
pestiferous farrago of filth.”” Again he says—‘ The
whole thing is evil to the last degree. Everyone is paw-
ing with vice, hinting at it, grinning at it, indulging
in it.”’

We have often spoken out about the English mind
in Ireland as it presents itself on the stage; we waged
2 war against filth on the Dublin stage, and that war
was partly effective. However, one weekly paper can-
not do everything. The Dublin daily papers have con-
tinued to draw a revenue by advertising imported
dramatic filth whenever it comes over here. The Free-
man, though supposed to he National and claiming to
be an organ of an Irish Catholic public, has never taken
up the cause of a clean stage; it has been whipped into
an odd half-hearted and palpably hypocritical protest,
though it never refused the advertisements of “ putrid
filth ”’ ; why do not some of the sharcholders of the
Freeman, who have consciences, raise this question of
puffing and advertising filthy plays at one of the meet-
f ings of the company? When the Archbishop of Dublin
% spoke out against the filthy stage some years ago, of
b course the Freeman ran up the Pastoral Kopje and

{ stayed on top until his Grace’s back was turned when
it beat a nimble retreat to the base. His Lordship of
Limerick made short work of ‘‘ 8apho ’’ some time ago ;

i
i

problem, and if the Press is complaisant to imported

“ putrid filth”’ on the stage—particularly in those

times when so many are on an edge for spying out

examples of clerical interference—the ‘‘ putrid filth *’

will keep on being imported. The shareholders of the

Gaiety ought to examine their consciences—if they have
- any; and the conscientious shareholders of the Freeman
would do well to organise an anti-Putrid Filth -Com-
mittee in time to take action at :the next general
meeting.

GREAT NORTHERN RAILWAY.

7Y HE position of Catholics on the Gr_ea.t Northern

‘Railway continuesito be most unsa_tlsfactcry. In
Phree Railways and a Bank details are given, and from
these it appears that of £21,130 paid to clerks, efc., in
Amiens Street Station, Catholics received only £720—
cightpence in the pound.

‘At Belfast Terminus, Catholics got £895 out of
£7,862. In the station stall and locomotive staff
Catholics got about one-fourth of the salaries. With
one exception, no Catholic got more than £145 a year
in the G.N.R. In whole departments not one Catholic
was employed. In the Manager’s office, in the Secre-
tary’s, in the Cashier’s, in the Engineer’s office—nof,
one Catholic was employed.

A little time has elapsed since the LEADER’S returns
were prepared, but there has been no substantial change.
As the Chairman remarked at the late meeting—‘“When
persons enter our service they generally wish to re-
main.” Quite right—but the trouble is that Catholics
seldom get the chance of entering. This is carefully
hedged round, and the old Bandon motto might be
put up.

At the half-yearly meeting on Wednesday, 14th
February, there was a large attendance. As an ‘‘ addi-
tion ”’ to the Report Mr. Dolan, Co.C., Ardee, and Mr.
P. Plunkett, Palmerston Road, moved: —

“ That the Directors be requested to prepare a scheme
for throwing entrance to our Clerkships open to com-
petitive examination, somewhat after the manner of
the Civil Service and the Great Southern and the Mid-
land Railways.”

The motion was supported by Mr. M. Sullivan (Bel-
clare), Mr. Haugh, Dr. Magennis (Harcourt Street),
and others. It was not necessary to say much, for the
proposal was so reasonable that no serious argument
against it was brought forward. Perhaps the strongest
point—if it can be called strong—was made by the
Chairman, who said that the number of vacancies each
year was so small that it was not worth while to have
such an examination as was asked for! But he forgot
that the filling up of these vacancies fills up—step by
step—all the places in the Company’s gift; that these
amount to many hundreds who are paid many thousands
of pounds in salaries; and that as, practically, Catholics
are excluded from an entrance-examination, they are
thus excluded from the many valuable appointments
connected with the Company.

A vote was taken on this proposed amendment. This
was done by the primitive system of shouting ‘‘ aye
or ‘“No,” and it appeared to many that the ‘‘ Ayes ™
had it. The Chairman, however, adopting a practice
which is steadily growing, declared that his own side—
the ““ Noes ’—had it, and when the ‘“ Ayes’” demanded
a poll the Chairman refused.

After the voting the interest in the proceedings
flagged, and as soon as the outgoing Directors had been
re-elected most of the sharcholders left the room.

There remained a remnant to look after the three
Parliamentary Bills which the Directors were obliged to
lay before the shareholders. In general such proceed-
ings are purely formal, but in the present case they had
considerable interest.

One of the Bills proposed to transfer the Donegal
Railways to the Midland Railway of England and the
Great Northern of Ireland.

The Midland of England is steadily extending its
power in Iveland. In the present case it was proposed
to give the Midland the sole control of the second line
hetween Derry and Strabane, and to give a ‘‘ Joint
Committee ’ of the Midland (England) and the Great
Northern (Ireland) control over the remaining portions
of the Donegal Railway.

At present appointments to positions in the Donegal
Railways are made by the existing Board of Directors,
including a baronial representative, and, as far as we
know, this Board of Directors does not exclude Catholics.
The proposal ‘to hand over the management of the
Donegal Railways, including appointments, ‘to & Joint
Committee from the Great Northern and the English
Midland, seemed to be a proposal for extending the

Mr. Davitt has by this time rummaged through
all the iniquities of Bishops in every department
of life. But “ Catholic Democrat ’’ was threatening to
make things a little hot for him, and in his 3rd letter
he more or less kept to the question he originally
raised—the English School question. But he has not
improved matters for himself. He quotes words from
several priests in England to show that the Bill of
1902 was unsatisfactory. He need not have taken the
trouble; those words he quoted only tell us what every-
body knew before—i.e., everybody who knows anything
about the question. I cannot see why he took the
trouble to hunt up those extracts, unless that he
thought it may throw dust in our eyes.

Mr. Davitt, in the course of his ramblings from the
question, said that the Catholic Schools were being
cused to train up Irish children for the English army—
in fact, to turn them into Euglishmen. Of course,
-that is silly on the face of it; the managers of nearly
all those Schools, and the teachers, too, are as
thoroughly Irish as Mr. Davitt is; and none of them
would lend themselves to English Tory purposes, as he
lends himself to English Radical purposes. But the
funny thing about it is, that Mr. Davitt’s plan for
improving the situation is as follows—Let the Irish
.children in England enter the English Board Schools,
get mixed up with and lost in the midst of multitudes
.of English children, read out of their lesson books, be
taught by their English teachers, drilled by their drill-
‘masters, learn everything that they learn, and live as
they live. That is Mr. Davitt’s method for protecting
the Irish character of Irish children in England. And
he is an Irish patriot! It looks as if he is more of a
British Radical. That is only one of the many con-
tradictions in Mr. Davitt’s letters. The public will

_hear more of them before I am done
I now proceed to show two things—1. That the pur-
:poss of Cardinal Manning was to get what the Bill of
1902 gave, although in an unsatisfactory way—namely,
.a share of the School Rates. 2. That the Nonconfor-
:mists had been aiming at ever since 1870 to do what
they proposo to do now. Mr. Davitt wants us to be-
“lieve that their present purpose has been determined
" by the action of the Catholics in connection with the
Bill of 1902. Nothing of the kind. They have been
“ playing that game since 1870. If they wanted to make
an exception of Catholic Schools, they had lots of time
to do it ; but they never did it, and never wanted to do
it, unless a few men like John Morley who would do it

. at onece.

THE LEADER. 7

Last week T gave a fow facts, which Mr. Davitt will
have to face if he wants to defend his fancies
before the public. I don’t expect he will answer me;
nor do I care whether. My purpose is to convince the
public, and I depend on facts to do that. The Irish
public  to-day is not what it was ten years ago.  The
people think now ; they separate facts from ‘fancies, and
reasoning from raimeis.  Before, when a popular man
like Mr. Davitt addressed them, they used to shut
their eyes and open their mouths; now they shut their
mouths and open their eyes. They are ceasing to mis-
take belabouring Bishops for fine speaking, and are be-
ginning to rummage through the rcason (or raimeis) of
popular orators to sce for themselves if there be any
holes in the oratory.

M. Davitt dare not deny the facts T gave last week ;
and it seems he knew very little about them. But he
says that the cause of the present attitude of the Non-
conformists towards the Catholic schools is to be laid at
the door of the English and Irish Bishops, and of the
Irish Party. In other words that, had it not been for
the action of the Irish Party in voting for the Bill of
1902, the Nonconformists might have let the schools
rest as they were. So well they might, indeed, since
they had the lion’s share of the booty. However, the
Catholics could not leave the matter rest; they meant
to fight for their rights against wrong. But
were the Nonconformists satisfied with the lion’s share?
Would they not have tried to force on the Education
scheme which they are now forcing if the Bill of 1902
had not been passed? Why their constant action since
1870 is the best proof that their action to-day is what
they were always aiming at. I could trace, as if inch
by inch, the inroads they have been making on Volun-
tary Schools since the Board Schools were established
in 1870. The Board Schools were originally meant
merely to supplement the Voluntary Schools; but the
purpose of the Nonconformists was to have the Board
Schools supplant the Voluntary Schools. Perhaps they
are using the action of the Irish Party as an excuse for
their present purpose. But it is only an excuse—and a
lame one, which is crippled by their action ever since
1870.  Probably they told it to Mr. Davitt, and he
took their word. For, I prefer to believe of him that
he has written in ignorance of the question than that
he wilfully sought,, by his letters in the Freeman, to
deceive the Irish public. T do not think that he wanted
to wilfully dupe them ; I think his greatest dupe in the
matber is himself.

He wrote in his 2nd letter : —* They (the Nonconfor-
mists) felt that the Catholics should have remained neu-
tral; should not have taken sides; should have fol-
lowed, instead, the wise policy of Cardinal Manning in
appealing for special and considerate treatment for the
Schools of the Irish poor in England to both political
parties, and not to become the partisans of either. That

MORALS, EMIGRATION AND THE WESTERN
STATES.

I HAVE been a constant reader of the LEADER since
the first copy, which breathed new life into a
+despairing and dying race. Needless to state, I am in
hearty sympathy with all the new and healthy move-
ments it has inaugurated, especially the campaign
-against emigration. On the latter topic I wish to state
a few facts which have come under my notice during
my wanderings through the western part of the United
-States. I am, myself, an exilo not by choice, but be-
-cause I was forced on account of ill health to scek a
batter climate than that of Ireland. Not to occupy
-_your valuable time with facts which are probably fami-
liar to you and readers of your paper, I will deal in this
letter with the most important aspect of the emigration
question—the moral effect on the emigrant. As has
been fully demonstrated in this and other lands, the
Irish emigrants are very easily and quickly assimilated ;
they readily accustom themselves to conditions and prac-
tices with which, in their old swrroundings, they were
not familiar. This characteristic, if a material, is not
always a moral advantage. Nowadays all through the
west the spirit of irreligion is rampant, and it is not un-
usual to find men and women of education talk as if
veligion were a sham, and those who practise it either
fools or knaves. Of course, the Catholic Church .is
singled out as the special object of atback of those
people raised in an atmosphere of bigotry, and taught
to hate all who belong to the Church of Rome. Look-
ing with contempt on religion- and religious practices, it
is easy to conceive how strong their inclination is to
observe the laws of God. The plain fact is that the
moral standard of the young men of the western cities
is extremely low, to speak mildly.  History repeats
itself ; conditions in the United States are not unlike
‘what existed in Rome and Italy in pre-Christian days.
The old rugged, Republican simplicity of living gave
place to the luxurious ease which prosperity brought
with it, and the most odious forms of vice were not
only indulged in by the people, but approved of by
their priests. However, I am not a pessimist, and I
feel sure that the sterling qualities of the American
will be proof against the evils which prosperity engen-
ders. I am not by any means blind to the splendid
qualities of mind and heart which the American pos-
sesses, but my task here is to give a true picture of
some of the conditions which the emigrant must face.
Every mining camp and small town of the west has its
red-light district, inhabited by prostitutes, the scum of
American and European society. In the large towns
they are licensed, and contribute to the revenue of the
city—a city, in other words, deriving part of its re-
venue from the sale of these unfortunate women’s souls.
The greatest problem confronting every Christian
mother here is how to raise her children so as to keep
“them pure and untainted by the awful depravity which
.exists in their midst. This state of things exists
amongst Catholics as well as non-Catholics. ~ I have
met with hundreds of young men whose parents were
good, practical Catholics, and yet the moral status of
“these young fellows was no higher than that of an un-
-civilised pagan. These are the men amongst whom the
Irish emigrant’s lot is cast; these are the men with
whom he must perforce associate. And what is the
‘result? I am open to correction, but I say without
hesitation that fifty per cent. of the young men from
Treland become as utterly degraded as the native Ame-
‘rican. I have spent four years amongst the farms and
.cattle ranches, and yet this disgusting evil is every-
-where met with. The early writings of President Roose-
velt tell of the bestial excosses indulged in by the cow-
“boys and the cattle-owners whenever they reach a town.
‘However, the Irishman, as a rule, steers clear of the
“farms and ranches, city life being far more attractive.
Men, prominent in social and business circles indulge
in the lowest forms of semsuality. Married men with
grown-up families frequent the dives and evil resorts,
.and their sons follow their example unchecked by
arental authority. Association in business and social
fe with such people conduces to the moral degradation

AN EPITAPH.

HE tall, thin man with the tired face and wegdy
whiskers was a journalist. Your eye, travelling
upwards, saw first a pair of bulgy boots, then a pair of
trousers with baggy knees; a frock coat that once was
new and fashionable in a day that was neither yester-
day nor the day before; long straggling whiskers, and a
seedy top hat surmounting all. He looked jaded, dis-
couraged, dispirited. His glance fell indifferently on
the tomb-stones. He was not thinking of them. For
the moment his mind was as empty of thought as his
heart of hope or zest. He looked, as he felt, old and
tired and indifferent.

His companion was in striking contrast. From his
dapper boots to his soft hat of good make his attire was
neat and spruce and smart. His snub nose carried gold-
rimmed glasses, through which quick blue eyes looked
out restlessly.  His clean-shaven face was a healthy
pink, and his abundant hair a bright red. Irish face
and American clothes told you much about him at one
look. Of one age with the tall man by his side, he yet
looked ten years younger. Saloon-keeping in the States
was evidently a more flourishing business than sub-edit-
ing an English provincial journal.

The American was home on holiday, after nearly
forty years away. He had come over from Ireland to
see his old friend and schoolmate, and together they
had journeyed to London to look up yet another friend
there settled. And now they were treading a gravelled
path in the little Campo Santo of London Irish Catho-
licism—St. Patrick’s at Leytonstone. A silent visit
they were paying this time to ome who could not bid
them welcome.

The tall man stopped at a turn in the path, and
stepped lightly along the foot of a few green mounds.

“There,”” said he, pointing to where a massive stone
was reared, “ there's another of life’s little ironies. Or
perhaps I should say death’s little ivonies. ~ What do
you think of that for a headstone for poor Pat?’

The American leant over and looked long at the in-
scription. In deeply-cut letters the wayfarer was asked
to “ pray for the soul of Patrick Joseph Dempsey, First
Class Examining Officer of Her Majesty’s Customs.”
He had been told what he would read, yet he
straightened himself now, drawing in his breath and
clicking his tongue sharply against the roof of his
mouth, with the air of one who sees a strange and sur-
prising thing. His pink face was wrinkled with a smile
he did his best to suppress, feeling it to be somehow
indecorous. e sighed a little, and shook his head,
though his smile had not vanished, as one who reflects
upon the vanity of things.

“Poor Pab!” he echoed. “The light of heaven to
him!”*  Then, with a quick turn of his head in the
other’s divection, he broke out sharply—* What an
epitaph for Pat Dempsey! Poet, patriot, ardent Fenian
forty years ago, and now—" First Class Examining Offi-
cer of Her ‘Majesty’s Customs.” No harm to say life
sees some queer changes.”’

“ Aye, indeed,” assented his companion indifferently.
He, too, was looking at the inseription, the pallid ghost:
of a whimsical smile lighting his face faintly. Then
with more animation he went on— There was no essen-
tial change in Pat, though—not an iota. He’s not re-
sponsible for that thing’’—pointing to the headstone.

our opponents and critics are so often in the wrong,
though T might not always have perceived it myself;
but when I find that they are always in the wrong, T
begin to grow uneasy, and to wonder why it should be
always night in their minds, and always day in ours.
But everybody is not as I am, and many of our good
people appear to enjoy a blessed immunity from mis-
givings which would give me great ease of mind, could
I only acquire it. And now for an interlude.

Some twenty months ago Mr. John Morley, in an
address delivered at some function, touched on the
broader aspects of culture and education.  Culture
he described as something that produces ‘liberty of
mind,” meaning, I think, deliverance from prejudice,
conventional opinion, and the snares of one’s own
mental idiosyncrasies. ~ He said that the difference
between the educated and the half-educated lies in the
freer recogmition of truth by the former. ¢ Freer and
fuller,”” T would have said, but that is a detail. A
writer in the Saturday Review wrote an admirable little
essay on the Morley address, and called it “ Amici
Veritatis.”” Amongst other things he said:—“1It is
the fate of the ignorant not to know the truth. It is
the fate of the educated to sophisticate it.”” He went
on to remark that there is probably no man or woman
who does not shrink from knowing all the truth about
some class of facts or other. In this I concur. We all
know how Don Quixote, when about to set forth on his
knightly adventures, tested his old helmet, and found
it very vulnerable. So he repaired the damage in-
flicted by the test, and—refrained from testing it any
further! We are all like the poor Don in that, and
must not laugh at him without having a quiet smile at
ourselves, too; we all shrink from knowing the wlole
truth about some one thing or other—more than one,
mayhap. T fear, too, that in Ireland this very human
peculiarity is accentuated, rather than absent. I fear
that when we “refute” hostile critics we are simply
restoring the old helmet to its former condition—not
making it a good, sound helmet, fit to face the wars
of the world.

But, education?  Well, I think the “ward” o1
direction of my views on education may be inferred
from the foregoing interlude. I am not writing a
formal treatise, observe, and wish to set down my
thoughts in my own way. Education should so liberate
a man that he should not be ““ passion’s slave *’ nor the
timid waiter upon conventional opinion, nor a weak
acquiescer in the tyrannous unanimity of those who
think least. A writer lately said something in these
pages about the possibility of reading Plunkett’s book
without swallowing it. Now, I should say that the
mental state which one infers from that phrase implies
education—that is, a condition of mind in which the
powers have been ‘“drawn out’’—i.c., educated.
Again, Henri F. Amiel has written of ““that inner
freedom of the soul (or “mind,” if one will), that
profound harmony of all the faculties”’ which, he says,
hie has observed in the best Germans. If that be edu-
cation, I fear that some eminent members of Trinity
" College are hardly educated—not, certainly, in the
sense in which Aristotle was educated.

Education is not really a thing, but a condition of
things mental. If you will, it is a process—the process
of freeing the faculties. Education, too, is an instru-
ment of wisdom; or, if you like, an eye by which we
look into the well of truth. It must not be confounded
with learning, nor with erudition. These things help
towards enriching the mind of an educatéd man—to-
wards completing and arming him; they are not in
themselves education. There have been eruditer men
who were really uneducated, and there have been edu-
cated men without erudition. Cobden was not erudite,
but he was éducated ; Salmasius was not educated, but
lie was erudite. Erasmus was both erudite and edu-
cated. In the earlier pages of Ruskin’s “Sesame and
Lilies ”” occur some eloquent passages on another
aspect of education. I need not cite them here. In-
deed, I think I have already said enough to show in a
general way what I conceive education should be, and
what should be its chief effeots.. The proneness to con-

found education with learning, or erudition, was cha-

racteristic of the past; the tendency to confound it
with mere instruction was very prevalent everywhere

in the middle of last century, as it is in Ireland still. .

There is now a tendency to confuse education with
tochnical training, expertism, and specialisation—all
of which I hold to be a mistake. Education ‘is some-
thing which frees the faculties, and opens the mind;

something that gives us the power of showing intellec- -

tual hospitality to other minds without abasing our
own. It gives us the power of suspending judgment,
of discussing things freely, of differing temperately,
and of taking triumphs moderately. These arc some of
the best effects of education. IMAAL.

HE Lord Justice has quoted the three following
rules for Schoolmasters and Ushers:—

“1. None are to be admitted Schoolmasters of said
Schools but such as are of the Protestant religion.

“ 9. The Schoolmaster and, in his absence, the Usher,
shall publickly, every morning, read a chapter out of
the Canonical Scriptures, and then pray, concluding at
night also with prayer. This to be done in the Englisk
tongue that all may be edified; they are to urge the
children frequently to read the Scriptures, and at con-
venient times to require some account of what they
read, according to their capacity, and that the meanest
of them be able, readily, to give an account how many
books, and the order in which they stand, as also the
number of chapters in each book, both of the Old and
New Testaments.

“3. The weakness of children is such that many
times variety of Catechisms confounds their under-
standings, and the Lord Primate Usher’s Catechism
being specially commended to those Schools in the Char-
ter, the Masters are diligently and constantly to cate-
chise them in that form. And, on the Lord’s Day.
before or after publick worship, to expound the sams,
or part thereof, unto them, as also at other convenient
times.””

Of these rules the Lord Justice has said:—* With
the framing, the publication, or the enforcement of
these supplemental rules, the Government or the law
is in no way brought into privity; they are the spon-
taneous act of Erasmus Smith himself, exercising the
power which the Charter conferred upon him, of lay-
ing down rules which no one could afterwards vary or
suspend.”’

A more extraordinary misstatement than this never
fell from the lips of man; there is not a particle of
truth in it from beginning to end.

Not only has “the Government and the law been
brought into privity with the framing, the publication,
and the enforcement of those rules,” but each of them
is a distinct enactment of the law of the land, and of
the law of the Protestant Church in Ireland.

I have already proved that the law of the land, and
the law of the Protestant Church in Ireland, had com-
manded that Protestants only should be Schoolmasters—
2.e.,, had commanded the first rule.’

Thm:e is a strong probability that the first rule has
been interpolated. It looks strange that Erasmus
Smith should have made a special rule that the Masters
should bo Protestants, when the law commanded that
only Protestants should be Masters, and punished
severely persons of any other denomination who acted
as Schoolmasters. The meaning of the rule is:—“The
Schoolm.:xstcrs are not to break the law.”” Did Eras-
mus Smith make such a rule? Moreover, such a rule
was unnecessary ; for, as I have shown, the Letters
Patent and the Charter had made special provision that
only Episcopalian Protestants could be Schoolmasters
of the Schools.

I shall prove that another of the rules has been in-
terpolated for a special purpose by the Governors. The
Report of the Commission of 1857 tells us that the

Governors kept the rules from that Commission until

Lord Justice Fitzgibbon, Rep. 1894, p. 238, has
said of Erasmus Smith:—The whole history of his
life shows that Erasmus Smith was an ecarnesf Protes-
tant, that it was his intention to propagate his faith

* by his schools, that the only bouuty which he ever
offered to anybody was Protestant education.””

These three statements are false.  As to the first,
that he was “ an earnest Protestant,”” the Lord Justice,
in another part of his judgment, quotes Rev. Dr.
Nicholas with approbation as saying that ho was “en-
thusiastically a Protestant.”” I will not dispute with the
Lord Justice and Dr. Nicholas as to what precise type
_of humanity “an earnest and enthusiastic Protestant '’
is, or might be. But if Erasmus Smith was “an
carnest and enthusiastic Protestant,”” the Vicar of
Bray was one. He was an anti-Episcopalian under
Cromwell; he was an Episcopalian under Charles
IL.; if James had defeated William at the
Boyne, he would have been a Catholic under James;
if the Sultan of Turkey ruled these countries, he would
have become a Mahomedan under him, if by this
change, or by any of the previous changes of religion,
he could have retained his ill-gotten goods.

I have shown that his intention was to get land by
his Schools, not to propagate his faith. What was his
faith? If this statement of the Lord Justice be true,
he sought to propagate two faiths diametrically op-
posed—anti-Episcopalianism and Episcopalianism. The
third statement is also false. For he offered his
tenants and others free secular education, and the State

added Protestantism to it under Charles II., and added.
Puritanism to it under Cromwell. He had no choice
as to this addition. If he gave education at all under
Cromwell, ho should have given Puritan education; if
he gave education at all under Charles II., he should
have given Protestant education. And this was true
in both cases, whatever his own religious convictions
might have been. 8o that the Lord Justice’s and Dr.
Nicholas’s speculations about his religious opinions,
oven if they were true, would be altogether beside ‘thc
question, for he should have impressed the Puritan
character on his Endowment under Cromwell, and
the Protestant character on it under Charles II.  Lord
Justico Fitzgibbon has said in his judgment, Rep. 1894,
p. 244:—“T think he used the word Protestant to
cmbrace all Protestants, including Presbyterians and
Independents, and that he did not regard the term as
exclusively denoting the members of the Ipiscopal Es-
tablished Church.”” Therefore, according to the Lord
Justice, when he wrote in his letter of 1682 :— My end
in founding the three Schools was to propagate the Pro-
testant faith,” he said, that, among other ends, his.
end was to propagate the Puritan faith in Ireland;
aud he thus told King Charles II. that his end was to
propagate the faith of the men who had murdered his
father; to propagate the faith which he, Charles II.,
had suppressed and forbidden under severe penalties.
And he thus told the world at large that his end was to
propagate two faiths diametrically opposed—anti-Epis-

clusions have the dexterous polemics of the Lord Jus-
tice irresistibly led.
